document:
# Overview

The auth-service issues and verifies session credentials for every internal tool. It is written in Rust and deployed as a sidecar. Throughput is around forty thousand requests per second at the edge.

# Tokens

The API uses PASETO tokens, not JWT, because PASETO removes the algorithm-confusion footgun. Access tokens live for fifteen minutes and refresh tokens for thirty days. Rotation of the signing key happens every twenty-four hours.

# Storage

Signing secrets live in the vault under the auth mount. They are never written to git or to environment files on disk. Access is brokered by a short-lived AppRole that the sidecar renews on a lease.

# Failure modes

If the vault is unreachable the service fails closed and returns a 503, rather than minting unsigned tokens. A circuit breaker trips after five consecutive lease-renewal errors.

questions.q: what token format does the auth service use
where are the signing secrets kept
what does the service do when the vault is down

document:
# Platform

Production runs on a managed Kubernetes cluster in three availability zones. Rollouts are progressive with a ten percent canary for fifteen minutes.

# Volumes

The database uses an attached block volume mounted at /data. At boot the entrypoint chowns the root-owned volume and then drops privileges with gosu before starting the server. This avoids running the app as root.

# Secrets

Deploy-time secrets come from the platform secret store and are injected as tmpfs files, never baked into the image layer.

# Rollback

A failed health check inside the canary window triggers an automatic rollback to the previous revision within ninety seconds.

questions.q: how does the container drop root privileges at startup
where is the database volume mounted
what happens when a canary fails its health check

document:
# Model

The memory engine stores four memory types: working, episodic, semantic, and procedural. Each has its own scoring profile and lifecycle.

# Decay

Retention follows an Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, exp of minus elapsed days over stability. Reinforcement on access raises stability with a spacing effect, so frequently used memories decay more slowly.

# Recall

Recall fuses three arms with reciprocal rank fusion: dense vectors, a lexical BM25 index, and a personalized PageRank walk over the knowledge graph. A cross-encoder then reranks the fused candidates.

# Grounding

Grounded recall cites its sources and abstains when the support score falls below a floor, rather than fabricating an answer.

questions.q: what forgetting curve models memory decay
which algorithm walks the knowledge graph during recall
what does grounded recall do when support is low

document:
# Plans

There is a free tier plus paid Pro and Team plans. Team uses native seat pricing billed per active seat.

# Metering

Only net-new content is metered; re-storing identical content is deduplicated at write time and costs nothing. Repeated syncs of the same document do not redraw from the balance.

# Webhooks

The billing service consumes order.paid and subscription.updated events and reconciles seat counts mid-cycle.

# Refunds

Refunds are issued as balance credits that never expire, distinct from monthly subscription credits which reset.

questions.q: how is repeated identical content billed
how is the team plan priced

document:
# Accounts

New hires get accounts provisioned automatically from the HR feed on their start date. Access to production requires a separate approval.

# Laptops

Standard issue is a developer laptop imaged with the base toolchain. Full-disk encryption is mandatory and enforced by policy.

# Payroll

Salary is paid monthly on the last banking day. Expense reimbursements run on a separate fortnightly cycle. The unique payroll reference is CHRONOSPAY.

# Mentorship

Every new engineer is paired with a mentor for the first ninety days and has a standing weekly check-in.

questions.q: when are salaries paid each month
what is the payroll reference code
is disk encryption required on laptops

document:
# Ingestion

Raw events land in an object store and are compacted hourly into columnar Parquet partitions keyed by day and tenant.

# Transform

The transform layer is idempotent: re-running a partition produces the same output, so backfills are safe. Late-arriving data is merged with a watermark of two hours.

# Quality

Every partition passes a schema check and a row-count anomaly check before it is published. A failing partition is quarantined, never published half-written.

# Serving

Downstream marts read only published partitions through a view that hides quarantined data.

questions.q: what is the late-arrival watermark in the transform step
what happens to a partition that fails a quality check
what file format are events compacted into
